title: GABA-containing neurons in the rat ventral tegmental area project to the prefrontal cortex
abstract:
GABA-Containing Neurons in the Rat
Ventral Tegmental Area Project to the
Prefrontal Cortex
DAVID B. CARR AND SUSAN R. SESACK*
Departments of Neuroscience and Psychiatry, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
KEY WORDS
prefrontal cortex; prelimbic cortex; infralimbic cortex; ventral tegmen-
tal area; GABA; dopamine
ABSTRACT
Dopamine-containing projections from the ventral tegmental area
(VTA) to the prefrontal cortex (PFC) have been extensively characterized since their
discovery over 25 years ago. However, the VTA projection to the PFC also contains a
substantial nondopamine component, whose neurochemical phenotype is unknown. To
examine if a portion of this nondopamine VTA projection contains GABA, anterograde
and retrograde tract-tracing in the rat was combined with GABA immunocytochemistry
and electron microscopy. Following injections of Fluoro-Gold (FG) into the PFC, many
VTA neurons were retrogradely labeled, as visualized by immunoperoxidase staining for
FG. A large portion of FG-labeled somata (58%) and dendrites (33%) within the VTA also
contained immunogold-silver labeling for GABA. These dually labeled proﬁles exhibited
a morphology similar to dopamine-containing cells within the VTA. To conﬁrm and
extend these ﬁndings, anterograde transport of biotinylated dextran amine (BDA) from
the VTA was combined with immunogold-silver labeling for GABA within the PFC.
Consistent with the results obtained from retrograde tracing, a portion of BDA-labeled
terminals in the PFC also contained immunoreactivity for GABA. These dually labeled
terminals formed symmetric synapses onto small caliber dendrites and dendritic spines.
Some PFC dendrites contacted by GABA-containing VTA terminals were themselves
GABA-labeled. The results of this investigation have identiﬁed a substantial population
of GABA-containing neurons in the VTA that send axons to the PFC where they synapse
on the distal processes of both pyramidal and local circuit neurons. This GABA-contain-
ing mesocortical pathway may provide substrates for both inhibitory and disinhibtory
inﬂuences on PFC neuronal activity. Synapse 38:114–123, 2000.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
INTRODUCTION
Ascending dopamine (DA) afferents from the ventral
tegmental area (VTA) play important roles in such
diverse neural functions as attention, motivation, re-
ward, and learning and memory (Kalivas and Nemer-
off, 1988). In addition to this well-characterized DA-
containing cell population, the VTA also contains a
substantial number of non-DA projection neurons that
may also contribute to these functions (Swanson,
1982). The neurochemical phenotype of these non-DA
VTA neurons is not known, although several lines of
evidence suggest that at least a portion of the non-DA
projections from the VTA contains GABA. Anatomical
studies have identiﬁed a population of GABA cells in
the VTA (Bayer and Pickel, 1991; Kalivas, 1993; Ko-
saka et al., 1987; Mugniani and Oertel, 1985; Nagai et
al., 1983) and have estimated that they comprise 15–
20% of the total neuronal population (Kalivas, 1993;
Mugniani and Oertel, 1985). However, these early an-
atomical studies did not determine whether this
GABA-containing population consists of projection
neurons, interneurons, or some combination of both
cell types. A more recent ultrastructural study by Van
Bockstaele and Pickel (1995) demonstrated that a pop-
ulation of VTA neurons, retrogradely labeled from the
nucleus accumbens (NAc), also contains GABA.
Compared to its projection to the NAc, the VTA pro-
jection to the prefrontal cortex (PFC) contains an even
larger population of non-DA neurons (Deutch et al.,
1991; Swanson, 1982). However, to date it has not been
Contract grant sponsors: Scottish Rite, NARSAD, USPHS; Contract grant
number: MH50314
*Correspondence to: Susan R. Sesack, Ph.D., Department of Neuroscience, 446
Crawford Hall, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA 15260.
E-mail: Sesack@bns.pitt.edu
Received 24 September 1999; Accepted 29 December 1999
SYNAPSE 38:114–123 (2000)
© 2000 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 determined whether a portion of this non-DA mesocor-
tical projection contains GABA. Physiological data
from Thierry and colleagues (Pirot et al., 1992) suggest
that such a projection may exist. For example, follow-
ing either neurochemical lesion of the ascending DA
ﬁbers or suppression of DA synthesis, VTA stimulation
produced inhibitory responses in PFC neurons that
could not be blocked by DA receptor antagonists, but
could be blocked by the GABAA antagonist bicuculline
(Pirot et al., 1992). While not direct evidence, these
ﬁndings suggest that a GABA-containing projection
from the VTA to the PFC modulates cortical activity.
In the present study, we combined anterograde and
retrograde tract-tracing methods with dual-label im-
munocytochemistry and electron microscopy to deter-
mine whether GABA-containing VTA neurons project
to the PFC. The results of this investigation have been
previously presented in preliminary form (Sesack and
Carr, 1998).
MATERIALS AND METHODS
FG injection and immunocytochemical
visualization
Fluoro-Gold (FG; Fluorochrome, Englewood, CO)
was stereotaxically injected into the medial PFC of
three adult male Sprague-Dawley rats anesthetized
with chloral hydrate (420 mg/kg, i.p.). The coordinates
for bilateral FG injection were 3.0 mm anterior to
bregma, 0.7 mm lateral to the midline, and 3.5 and 4.5
mm ventral to the skull surface, according to the atlas
of Paxinos and Watson (1986). Approximately 100 nl of
FG (1% in 0.1 M cacodylate buffer) was pressure-in-
jected at each of the four locations through glass pi-
pettes (tip diameter, 50 mm) over 10 min.
Following a 7-day survival period, animals were
deeply anesthetized with sodium pentobarbital (100
mg/kg i.p.) and perfused through the aortic arch with
10 ml of 0.9% saline containing 1,000 U/ml of heparin,
followed by 50 ml of 3.75% acrolein in 2% paraformal-
dehyde and 300 ml of 2% paraformaldehyde in 0.1 M
phosphate buffer, pH 7.4 (PB). The brains were re-
moved, and 4–5 mm coronal blocks containing the PFC
and VTA were stored in the last ﬁxative for 45 min.
Vibratome sections (40–50 mm) from these blocks were
collected in PB and treated in 1% sodium borohydride
in PB for 30 min to enhance immunocytochemical la-
beling (Leranth and Pickel, 1989).
A dual-labeling method was employed in which VTA
neurons containing retrogradely transported FG were
visualized by peroxidase immunocytochemistry, while
GABA neuronal phenotype was visualized by preem-
bedding immunogold labeling with silver intensiﬁca-
tion. Brieﬂy, sections were incubated for 30 min in
blocking solution containing 1% bovine serum albumin
and 3% normal goat serum in 0.1 M tris-buffered sa-
line, pH 7.6 (TBS). To enhance antibody penetration,
the blocking solution contained Triton X-100 (Sigma,
St. Louis, MO) at 0.04% for electron microscopic exam-
ination or 0.2% for light microscopy. Sections were
incubated for 12–15 h with polyclonal antiserum raised
in rabbits against FG (1:5,000, Chemicon, Temecula,
CA) and mouse anti-GABA monoclonal antibody (1:1,000,
Sigma) diluted in blocking solution. All incubation and
washing steps were performed at room temperature
under constant gentle agitation.
For peroxidase localization of FG, sections were
rinsed thoroughly in TBS and incubated for 30 min in
biotinylated goat antirabbit antiserum (1:400, Jackson
ImmunoChemicals, West Grove, PA) diluted in block-
ing solution. Sections were rinsed again, then incu-
bated for 30 min in avidin-biotin peroxidase complex
(1:200, Vectastain Elite Kit, Vector Labs, Burlingame,
CA). The peroxidase reaction product was visualized by
placing the sections in a solution containing 0.022%
diaminobenzidine and 0.003% hydrogen peroxide in
TBS for 3–4 min. The peroxidase reaction was termi-
nated by rinsing the sections in TBS. For immunogold
localization of GABA, sections were incubated for 30
min in a blocking solution containing 0.8% bovine se-
rum albumin and 0.1% ﬁsh gelatin in 0.01 M phosphate
buffered saline (PBS). Sections were then incubated in
blocking solution that also contained goat antimouse
antiserum conjugated to 1 nm gold particles (1:50, Am-
ersham, Cleveland, OH). Following rinses in blocking
solution and PBS, the size of the gold particles was
subsequently enhanced by incubation in silver solution
(Amersham) for 5–7 min.
For electron microscopic examination, sections were
postﬁxed for 1 h in 2% osmium tetroxide in PB, dehy-
drated through successive alcohols and propylene ox-
ide, and embedded in epon (EM bed 812, Electron Mi-
croscopy Sciences, Ft. Washington, PA). Sections were
mounted on epon blocks and ultrathin sections were
cut from the outer surface of the tissue and collected
onto copper mesh grids. Sections were counterstained
with uranyl acetate and lead citrate and examined
with a Zeiss 902 transmission electron microscope.
BDA injection and visualization
Biotinylated dextran amine (BDA, 10,000 MW, Mo-
lecular Probes, Eugene, OR; 10% in 10 mM phosphate
buffer) was stereotaxically injected into the VTA of two
adult male Sprague-Dawley rats anesthetized with
chloral hydrate (420 mg/kg, i.p.). The coordinates for
bilateral BDA injection were 3.3 mm anterior to
lambda, 1.6 and 2.1 mm lateral to the midline, and 7.6
mm ventral to the skull surface according to the atlas
of Paxinos and Watson (1986). BDA was iontophoreti-
cally injected for 20 min at each of the four locations
through glass micropipettes (tip diameter, 50 mm) low-
ered at a 10° angle in the coronal plane.
Following a 14–20 day survival period, animals were
anesthetized and perfused as described above. Tissue
sections from the VTA and PFC were processed for
GABA PROJECTIONS FROM VTA TO PFC

BDA injection and visualization
Biotinylated dextran amine (BDA, 10,000 MW, Mo-
lecular Probes, Eugene, OR; 10% in 10 mM phosphate
buffer) was stereotaxically injected into the VTA of two
adult male Sprague-Dawley rats anesthetized with
chloral hydrate (420 mg/kg, i.p.). The coordinates for
bilateral BDA injection were 3.3 mm anterior to
lambda, 1.6 and 2.1 mm lateral to the midline, and 7.6
mm ventral to the skull surface according to the atlas
of Paxinos and Watson (1986). BDA was iontophoreti-
cally injected for 20 min at each of the four locations
through glass micropipettes (tip diameter, 50 mm) low-
ered at a 10° angle in the coronal plane.
Following a 14–20 day survival period, animals were
anesthetized and perfused as described above. Tissue
sections from the VTA and PFC were processed for
GABA PROJECTIONS FROM VTA TO PFC
detection of BDA and GABA as described above, using
only the avidin-biotin peroxidase complex for BDA lo-
Ultrathin sections were taken from Vibratome sec-
tions through either the VTA, in cases of retrograde
transport, or the deep layers of the medial PFC, in
cases of anterograde transport. Within single, noncon-
secutive thin sections, tissue contained within grid
squares (boundaries of the grid mesh 5 3,025 mm2)
along the epon–tissue interface was examined at a
magniﬁcation of 20,0003. A total of 380,393 mm2 of
tissue was examined from animals receiving FG injec-
tions (3–4 Vibratome sections through the VTA per
animal), and 321,406 mm2 of tissue was examined from
through the PFC per animal). To reduce false-negative
results due to inadequate antibody penetration, only
those areas at the surface of the tissue (epon/tissue
interface) where antibody penetration is maximal were
examined. In addition, only micrographs that con-
tained both peroxidase and gold markers within the
same 32.5 mm2 area (area contained within a single
micrograph taken at 12,0003 magniﬁcation) were an-
alyzed. Finally, in tissue processed for immunogold-
silver localization of GABA, proﬁles were classiﬁed as
GABA-labeled if they contained $3 gold particles
within 2–3 consecutive serial sections in the case of
terminals or small caliber dendrites (,7 mm diameter)
or $10 gold particles for more proximal dendrites and
somata. These sampling methods have been exten-
sively used to study PFC and VTA tissue containing
both peroxidase and gold-silver markers (Sesack et al.,
1995a, b, 1998; Sesack and Pickel, 1992a,b).
Neuronal elements, as described by Peters et al.
(1991), were identiﬁed in electron micrographs as fol-
lows. Axon terminals were at least 0.2 mm in diameter
and contained numerous synaptic vesicles. Somata
were identiﬁed by the presence of a nucleus. Dendritic
and/or rough endoplasmic reticulum, exhibited densi-
ties postsynaptic to axon terminals, and lacked the
dense undercoating associated with axon initial seg-
ments. Dendritic spines also contained a postsynaptic
density but did not contain the above-mentioned or-
ganelles. Asymmetric synapses (Gray’s type 1; Gray,
1959) were identiﬁed by thickened postsynaptic densi-
ties, while symmetric synapses (Gray’s type II) had
Injections of FG were centered in the prelimbic and
infralimbic divisions of the medial PFC (Fig. 1A) ac-
cording to the cytoarchitecture of Krettek and Price
(1977). Following FG injections, labeled neurons were
found distributed throughout the parabrachial and
paranigral regions of the anterior VTA, consistent with
previous observations (Deutch et al., 1991; Swanson,
1982). At the light microscopic level, labeling for FG
was observed in neuronal somata as well as proximal
and distal processes of VTA neurons (Fig. 1B). At the
ultrastructural level, immunoperoxidase labeling for
FG was observed in neuronal somata as well as within
large and small caliber dendritic processes (Figs. 2, 3).
Peroxidase labeling was typically distributed through-
out the cytoplasm of labeled proﬁles; however, in some
cases FG labeling was contained almost exclusively
In tissue labeled for both FG and GABA, immuno-
gold-silver labeling for GABA was readily distinguish-
able from peroxidase labeling for FG and was diffusely
distributed throughout the cytoplasm and over nuclei.
GABA immunolabeling was observed in neuronal so-
mata (Fig. 2) and dendritic processes (Fig. 3) as well as
in terminals forming symmetric synaptic contacts. Nu-
merous somata and dendrites containing peroxidase
labeling for FG also contained gold-silver labeling for
GABA (Figs. 2, 3). Within the same area of tissue, other
dendrites and somata also contained peroxidase label-
ing for FG but were clearly immunonegative for GABA,
despite the presence of GABA-labeled proﬁles in the
adjacent neuropil (Figs. 2B, 3). Within the tissue ex-
amined, 58% of FG-labeled somata (61/105) and 33% of
FG-labeled dendrites (152/462) also contained immu-
nogold-silver labeling for GABA. These dually labeled
soma and dendrites exhibited morphological character-
istics similar to those of DA-containing cells within the
VTA (Bayer and Pickel, 1990; Domesick et al., 1983).
Dendrites either singly labeled for FG or GABA or
dually labeled for both markers received contacts from
terminals forming either asymmetric or symmetric
synapses. Some terminals forming symmetric synapses
contained gold-silver labeling for GABA (Fig. 3B). No
attempts were made in the present study to examine
possible differences in the density or type of synaptic
contacts onto singly or dually labeled VTA dendrites.
Dendrites were often surrounded by glial processes in
areas not contacted by synaptic terminals (see Fig. 3A).
Injections of BDA were centered in the anterior por-
tion of the VTA and included both medial and lateral
aspects (Fig. 1C). Following such injections, peroxidase
labeled axons and terminals were observed within the
PFC and adjacent NAc (Fig. 1D). Within the PFC,
layers V and VI contained the highest density of BDA
ﬁbers and terminals, with more scattered ﬁbers ob-
served in more superﬁcial layers. At the ultrastruc-
tural level, BDA-labeled terminals formed symmetric
synaptic contacts primarily onto small caliber den-
drites and dendritic spines. In tissue labeled for both
BDA and GABA, some terminals containing peroxidase
labeling for BDA also contained gold-silver labeling for
GABA (Fig. 4). In rare instances, these terminals were
adjacent to glial processes immunoreactive for GABA
(Fig. 4D). BDA/GABA-labeled terminals formed sym-
metric synaptic contacts onto both small caliber den-
drites and dendritic spines. The spines postsynaptic to
BDA/GABA-labeled terminals also received asymmet-
Fluorescence (A,B) and brightﬁeld (C,D) micrographs
showing representative injection sites and transport of tracers used in
this study. In A, a deposit of FG is centered within the medial PFC.
The FG injection is distributed throughout all layers between the pial
surface and the forceps minor (fm) with the heaviest deposit in layers
III and V. In B, many VTA neurons are intensely ﬂuorescent following
an injection of FG into the PFC. FG labeling also extends into the
dendrites of these cells (arrows). Medial is toward the left and dorsal
is toward the top. In C, four small iontophoretic injections of BDA
(arrows) are located within the VTA above the fasciculus retroﬂexus
(fr) and mammillary peduncle (mp). In D, BDA anterogradely trans-
ported from the VTA is present in highly varicose axons within both
the PFC and the adjacent nucleus accumbens (NAc). BDA-labeled
axons are concentrated within the deep layers of the cortex adjacent
to the forceps minor, with more scattered ﬁbers observed in the
superﬁcial layers. Scale bar 5 325 mm for A, 120 mm for B, 450 mm for
GABA PROJECTIONS FROM VTA TO PFC
Electron micrographs showing neuronal somata within the
VTA containing FG retrogradely labeled from the PFC. In A, a cell
body (FG/GABA-soma) contains immunoperoxidase labeling for FG
distributed throughout the cytoplasm and also heavily concentrated
within lysosomes (arrows). The soma also contains immunogold-silver
labeling for GABA. In B, a soma (FG-soma) contains dense peroxidase
labeling for FG but does not contain gold-silver labeling for GABA.
Some GABA-labeled dendrites (GABA-d) are visible in the adjacent
neuropil. In C, a soma (FG/GABA-soma) contains both peroxidase
labeling for FG exclusively within lysosomes (arrows) and gold-silver
labeling for GABA. Scale bar 5 2.0 mm.
Electron micrographs demonstrating the distribution of
immunoperoxidase labeling for retrogradely transported FG and im-
munogold-silver labeling for GABA within dendrites in the VTA. In A,
a dendrite contains both FG and GABA labeling and receives a syn-
aptic contact (curved arrow) from an unlabeled terminal (Ut). Within
the adjacent neuropil are processes singly labeled for GABA (GABA-d)
or FG (FG-d) or dually labeled for both markers (FG/GABA-d). These
proﬁles are likely to be dendrites, although one or more may be axon
initial segments. Some are surrounded on nonsynaptic sides by glial
processes (asterisks). In a lower magniﬁcation micrograph (B) a large-
caliber dendrite containing labeling for both FG and GABA (FG/
GABA-d) lies between two other large-caliber dendrites singly labeled
for either GABA (GABA-d) or FG (FG-d). The GABA-d is contacted by
several GABA-labeled terminals (arrowheads). In C, a small-caliber
dendrite dually labeld for FG and GABA (FG/GABA-d) lies adjacent to
a small dendrite singly labeled for FG (FG-d). Scale bar 5 0.5 mm in
GABA PROJECTIONS FROM VTA TO PFC
Electron micrographs from the PFC showing terminals
containing both immunogold-silver labeling for GABA and peroxidase
labeling for BDA anterogradely transported from the VTA (BDA/
GABA-t). In A a BDA/GABA-t forms a symmetric synapse (curved
arrow) onto an unlabeled dendrite (Ud). In B and C, a BDA/GABA-t
forms a symmetric synapse onto a smaller-caliber dendrite shown in
serial sections. In D, a BDA/GABA-t forms a symmetric synapse onto
a dendrite that also contains gold-silver labeling for GABA (GABA-d).
The dually labeled terminal lies adjacent to a glial process (asterisk)
that also contains gold-silver labeling for GABA and is connected to
another glial process via a tight junction (arrow). In E, an unlabeled
dendritic spine (Us) receives both an asymmetric synapse from an
unlabeled terminal (Ut) as well as a symmetric synapse from a BDA/
GABA-t. The dually labeled terminal is partially separated from the
unlabeled spine by a glial process (asterisk). Scale bar 5 0.25 mm.
ric synaptic input from unlabeled terminals (Fig. 4E).
Some dendrites contacted by BDA/GABA-labeled ter-
minals were themselves immunoreactive for GABA
(Fig. 4D). More precise quantiﬁcation of the proportion
of BDA-labeled terminals that also contained GABA or
the percentage of various synaptic targets was prohib-
ited by the rather limited penetration of immunogold-
silver labeling for GABA beyond the immediate tissue
The results of this investigation indicate that a sub-
stantial population of GABA-containing neurons in the
VTA send axons to the PFC, where they form symmet-
ric synapses on the distal processes of both pyramidal
GABA-containing pathway represents a previously un-
described modulatory projection from the mesence-
phalic tegmentum that may provide a substrate for
both inhibitory and disinhibtory inﬂuences on PFC
The relatively low density of immunogold-silver la-
beling observed within GABA somata and dendrites in
the VTA is consistent with other studies of GABA-
containing projection neurons within this region (Stef-
fensen et al., 1998; Van Bockstaele and Pickel, 1995) as
well as GABA projection neurons in other pathways
(Delle Donne et al., 1997; Van Bockstaele et al., 1996).
This low density of GABA immunoreactivity may be
responsible for the observed discrepancy in the propor-
tion of dually labeled somata and dendrites within the
VTA. Thus, the ﬁnding that fewer dendrites than soma
were dually labeled for FG and GABA is most likely
due to low levels of GABA in more distal processes,
although it cannot be ruled out that GABA cells have a
more limited dendritic tree than other VTA projection
neurons. In any case, the proportion of retrogradely
should probably be considered the better estimate of
the percentage of mesoprefrontal GABA neurons.
The low sensitivity of preembedding immunogold-
silver labeling for GABA also hampered attempts to
quantify the synaptic targets and the proportion of
VTA afferents containing GABA within the PFC. Ter-
minals immunoreactive for GABA were only observed
at the very surface of the tissue, increasing the proba-
bility of false-negative classiﬁcations. For this reason,
only qualitative observations of the postsynaptic tar-
gets of GABA-containing mesoprefrontal terminals are
presented here. Future studies utilizing more sensitive
methods such as postembedding immunostaining for
GABA will be required to estimate the proportion of
mesoprefrontal terminals that utilize GABA, as well as
the distribution of their synaptic targets. Nevertheless,
in the present study we have been able to establish the
existence of this important GABA projection from the
VTA and to identify the types of postsynaptic struc-
tures that it targets in the PFC.
Anatomical and functional considerations
The results of this study indicate that the majority
(;60%) of VTA projections to the PFC utilize GABA as
a neurotransmitter. In addition, the observation that
many PFC-projecting cells were immunonegative for
GABA is consistent with a previous investigation show-
ing that approximately one-third of this pathway con-
tains DA (Swanson, 1982). It is likely that DA- and
GABA-containing mesoprefrontal neurons represent
separate, rather than colocalized neuronal populations,
as Kosaka et al. (1987) have reported that DA and
GABA are not colocalized within VTA cells. It is not
known if the entirety of VTA projections to the PFC
utilize either DA or GABA or whether a third projec-
tion exists which utilizes another neurotransmitter. It
is also not known whether some GABA-containing me-
soprefrontal cells also contain neuropeptides. For ex-
ample, although all neurotensin-containing mesopre-
frontal neurons also contain DA (Seroogy et al., 1988),
some cholecystokinin (CCK) containing mesoprefrontal
cells do not contain DA (Seroogy et al., 1989). It is not
known whether these CCK neurons colocalize GABA or
represent a separate projection population containing
The presence of GABA projection cells intermixed in
a DA pathway appears to be a common feature of
several major ascending DA systems, including the
1999) and mesolimbic (Van Bockstaele and Pickel,
1995) pathways. Indeed, this feature appears to be
common for many cortically projecting neuromodula-
tory systems, as the basal forebrain cholinergic system
(Gritti et al., 1997), histaminergic projections from the
caudal hypothalamus (Staines and Nagy, 1991), and
possibly serotonergic projections from the raphe nuclei
(O’Hearn and Molliver, 1984; Van Bockstaele and
Pickel, 1993) all contain a GABAergic component.
Thus, in addition to the intrinsic inhibitory circuitry
provided by local circuit neurons, cortical neurons are
also subject to extrinsic GABA inputs from multiple
The functional signiﬁcance of the VTA GABA projec-
tions to the PFC remains to be determined. It is likely
that these GABA axons correspond to the 6-OHDA-
resistant, fast-conducting ﬁbers observed by Thierry et
al. (1980). This is consistent with our observations that
some of the GABA-containing VTA axons retrogradely
labeled from the PFC were myelinated (data not
shown). Studies examining the effects of VTA stimula-
tion on PFC neural activity have demonstrated that
the majority of the evoked inhibitory effects of VTA
stimulation on the spontaneous activity of PFC neu-
GABA PROJECTIONS FROM VTA TO PFC
rons can be blocked by administration of DA receptor
antagonists (Godbout et al., 1991; Pirot et al., 1992).
This is in apparent contradiction to our observation
that the majority of mesoprefrontal neurons contain
GABA, as well as previous data showing that only a
third of this pathway contains DA (Swanson, 1982).
This discrepancy may be due to a more limited termi-
nal arborization of GABA vs. DA containing mesocor-
tical axons within the PFC. Alternatively, although
both DA- and GABA-containing mesoprefrontal termi-
nals form synaptic contacts onto distal dendrites and
dendritic spines, the excitatory inputs that converge
onto these postsynaptic structures may differ. Prelim-
inary data from our laboratory suggests that DA ter-
minals may preferentially converge with excitatory ter-
neighboring PFC cells rather from extrinsic sources
(Carr and Sesack, 1996, 1998; Pinto and Sesack, 1998;
Sesack and Miner, 1997). If these intracortical excita-
tory connections are more important for driving spon-
taneous activity, this may explain the greater efﬁcacy
of DA afferents to modulate this activity.
In addition to contacts onto dendrites, mesocortical
GABA terminals also converged with unlabeled termi-
nals forming excitatory contacts onto dendritic spines.
In theoretical models, such inhibitory inputs to den-
dritic spines can modulate the efﬁcacy of excitatory
inputs, without signiﬁcantly altering the electrical ac-
tivity of the parent dendrites (Koch and Poggio, 1983;
Qian and Sejnowski, 1990; Rall and Segev, 1988; Segev
and Rall, 1988). Thus, such inputs to dendritic spines
may allow mesocortical GABA afferents to selectively
block excitatory inputs to pyramidal cells. Our previous
investigations suggest that there is a degree of selec-
tivity in the extent to which excitatory inputs onto
pyramidal cells are synaptically modulated at the level
of dendritic spines (Carr and Sesack, 1996, 1998).
Thus, GABA afferents from the VTA may target den-
dritic spines that receive a particular class of excitatory
inputs. For example, we have shown that GABA ter-
minals converge with callosal afferents onto common
targets within the PFC (Carr and Sesack, 1998) and it
is possible, though not yet tested, that these GABA
In addition to contacting the dendritic tree of pyra-
midal cells, dually labeled terminals also formed syn-
aptic contacts onto GABA-containing dendrites. Thus,
GABA afferents from the VTA may mediate a disin-
hibitory inﬂuence within the PFC through inhibition of
inhibitory local circuit neurons. Cortical interneurons
have been divided into multiple subtypes based on
their axonal morphology, as well as their content of
different calcium binding proteins (Gabbott et al.,
1997; Kubota et al., 1994). It is not known which sub-
classes of local circuit neurons receive GABA synaptic
contact from the VTA. However, given the different
roles each subclass is thought to play in cortical cir-
cuitry, the ultimate functional role of this mesocortical
GABA input to local circuit neurons will depend on the
In summary, these results represent the ﬁrst dem-
onstration of a substantial GABA-containing pathway
from the VTA to the PFC. Within the PFC, GABA-
containing VTA afferents synapse on distal portions of
the dendritic tree of both pyramidal and local circuit
neurons, where they likely mediate an inhibitory or
modulatory inﬂuence. Recent evidence suggests that
there is a reduced density of both DA ﬁbers and recep-
tors in the PFC of schizophrenic patients (Lewis and
Akil, 1997; Okubo et al., 1997). It is not known if the
GABA mesocortical pathway is similarly affected in
this disorder, although a reduction in this GABA in-
nervation may, at least in part, underlie the alterations
in GABA binding observed in the PFC of schizophrenic
patients (Benes et al., 1992, 1996). Alternatively, if the
GABA component of the mesocortical pathway is
spared, the resulting imbalance with the DA mesocor-
tical input may play a role in the hypofrontality asso-
ciated with this disorder (Carter et al., 1998; Wein-
berger et al., 1986). Future examination is required to
determine the role that this newly discovered GABA
pathway plays in both the normal functioning of the
PFC, as well as in disease states such as schizophrenia
and other neuropsychiatric disorders.
